name: TypeScript
analogy: Like spell-check for code — it catches mistakes before you run the program
whatItDoes: TypeScript is a superset of JavaScript that adds type annotations to your code. You declare what kind of data a variable holds — a string, a number, an object with specific fields — and TypeScript warns you immediately in your editor if you use it incorrectly. It compiles down to regular JavaScript so browsers and Node.js can run it.
whyYouUseIt: In SafetyIQ, incident records have many fields — site, severity, date, reporter name. TypeScript means I can define exactly what an incident object looks like and get an instant error if I try to save the wrong type of data to the database. This is especially important for an HSE app where data accuracy directly affects safety reporting.
whenToSwap: If you are building a very small script or prototype and want to move fast without setting up types, plain JavaScript is fine. JSDoc lets you add some type hints without switching languages. Flow is a Facebook alternative to TypeScript but has a much smaller community and ecosystem.
